title: Modern Cedar Fence Picket Birdhouse
summary: A clean-lined birdhouse from half of one resawn cedar picket — six flat panels, no roof pitch, no scrollwork, just a simple modern box a bird will actually use.
description:
Most decorative birdhouses are built for the porch, not the bird — elaborate rooflines and gingerbread trim that a cavity-nesting bird doesn't care about and a predator can perch on. This one goes the other direction: six flat cedar panels, square edges, and a small overhang on three sides for weather. It's a genuinely good beginner box-building project specifically because there's no angle anywhere in it — every cut is a straight crosscut.

The whole birdhouse comes from HALF of one cedar fence picket, resawn or planed down to 1/2" thick — considerably thinner than a picket's rough 5/8"-3/4" as-sold thickness, which is what gives this design its clean, modern proportions instead of the chunky look a full-thickness picket produces. If you don't have a way to resaw stock that thin, a table saw with the fence set carefully or a hand plane both get there; it's worth doing rather than skipping, since the wall thickness is a visible part of the design.

Assembly order matters for keeping the reveals even: sides to the front first, then the top and bottom onto that sub-assembly with all back edges kept flush — the overhang lands automatically at about 1/4" on the front and both sides, with no overhang at all on the back, which is what lets the house sit flush against a post or tree without a gap. The back goes on last, after which the entrance hole and a hanging hole both get cut through it.

Skip a perch on the front — cavity nesters like chickadees and wrens don't use one, and an unused perch is just a better foothold for anything trying to raid the nest.

tools.note: Resaws the picket down to 1/2" thick — a hand plane substitutes.
The entrance hole and hanging hole in the back panel.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, resawn to 1/2" thick
materials.note: Only half a picket is used — the other half covers a second birdhouse.

cutList.note: Extends past the box for mounting — the entrance and hanging holes go through this piece.

steps.title: Attach the sides to the front
steps.body: Attach the two side panels to the front panel with exterior glue and fasteners, forming a three-sided open box.
materials: Cedar fence picket, resawn to 1/2" thick

steps.title: Add the top and bottom
steps.body: Keep the back edges flush and attach the top and bottom panels to the sides and front. The overhang works out to about 1/4" on the front and both sides, with no overhang on the back — that's what lets the finished house sit flush against a mounting post.
materials: Cedar fence picket, resawn to 1/2" thick

steps.title: Attach the back and drill the holes
steps.body:
Attach the back panel to the main house assembly, then cut the entrance hole (sized to the species you're hoping to attract — 1-1/8" for chickadees and wrens is a common size) and a hanging hole through the extended top of the back panel.

Skip adding a perch — cavity-nesting birds don't use one, and it only gives predators an easier foothold.
materials: Cedar fence picket, resawn to 1/2" thick

images.alt: Small modern cedar birdhouse with clean flat panels
